title: Raising a water butt to improve flow and connect a hose
subtitle: Why elevation matters and how to build a stable stand from timber or blocks
excerpt: A water butt sitting on the ground provides barely enough pressure to fill a watering can from the tap, and a hosepipe fed from it runs at a trickle. Raising the butt 300-400 mm on a stable stand roughly doubles the usable pressure and allows a hose to flow with enough force to actually water plants.
sourceNotes: Water butt supplier installation guidance (Harcostar, Graf, Tom Chambers). Gravity-fed irrigation design principles for low-head systems.

glossaryTerms.definition: The water pressure created by the height of the water above the outlet point. For every 100 mm of elevation, head pressure increases by approximately 0.01 bar. A water butt raised 400 mm above ground level produces approximately 0.04 bar at the tap — enough to fill a watering can freely and supply a soaker hose.

content.text: A full 200-litre water butt weighs approximately 200 kg. The stand must be stable and level. Two materials work well: dense concrete blocks (each 440 x 215 x 100 mm solid, not hollow) or a sawn timber frame from 75 x 75 mm pressure-treated posts and 50 x 150 mm decking boards. The

available from a 400 mm elevation is modest but sufficient for a soaker hose or drip irrigation line at low flow rates. For a standard hosepipe with spray gun, mains pressure is needed; a gravity butt will only support low-pressure soaker irrigation.

Four solid concrete blocks stacked two-high (giving 200 mm elevation) under each corner of the butt base provide a stable platform. Ensure the base is level: use a spirit level and add a paving slab or concrete pad under each block stack if the ground is soft. For 400 mm elevation, use a third layer of blocks or combine with a timber platform on top.

A simple platform: two pairs of 75 x 75 mm pressure-treated posts, 400-500 mm tall, set in a rectangle matching the butt's base footprint. Connect posts with 50 x 150 mm horizontal rails top and bottom on each pair. Deck the top with 50 x 150 mm boards spaced so they support the butt base without flex. Brace diagonally with a 50 x 100 mm diagonal on each side to prevent racking. Check the fully loaded stand for stability before leaving unattended.
Connecting a hose for gravity irrigation

Replace the standard water butt tap with a 3/4" or 1/2" BSP tap that accepts a hose connector. Most water butt taps have a 3/4" male BSP thread. Connect a soaker hose or drip line directly. Lay the soaker hose flat at the same level or lower than the tap: any rise in the hose will reduce flow to zero once the hose elevation equals the water level in the butt.
